title: Bar exams and legal education (1)

primary_topics: Bar exams and legal education trilogy part 1 â€” September 7, 2014; opening column of three-part legal-education series
URL slug 'bar-exams-and-legal-education-1' uses plural 'exams' + parenthetical '(1)' (parts 2-3 use singular 'exam' + bare-digit suffix); annual bar exam on four Sundays of October
PRC supervises all professions in PH EXCEPT legal which is reserved by Constitution for SC; PRC-administered tests called 'licensure examinations' but lawyer-crucible called 'bar' exam
Bar/bench metaphor origin: 'courtroom railing or bar that separates the lawyers' table and the judges' bench from the seats used by the general public'
Opening-day fanfare (parades, motorcades, marching bands, cheering squads) banned by SC during last several years; University of Cebu gifts top-10 with brand-new cars

CJP's 1960 bar exam personal experience â€” waded alone in rain-induced knee-deep flood on R. Papa Street, Sampaloc Manila from FEU Hospital (where confined for intestinal flu) to University of the East exam venue

Dean Jovito R. Salonga's pep talk when CJP nearly quit + Salonga's January 22, 2008 FEU speech quote: 'CJ Panganiban could have landed first place (instead of sixth) had he not been sick and hospitalized during the first week of the 1960 bar exam'

Classmate comparison showing examiner's evaluation favored CJP's essayed opinions over classmate's more-impressive citation of authorities â€” 'placing in the top 10 is not always a reliable measure of achievement' aphorism

Bar exam 2014 methodology: 20% MCQ + 80% essay; two new subjects added (taxation + labor law); Bar Matter 1161 (June 8, 2004) reforms by retired Justices Vicente V. Mendoza + Ameurfina M. Herrera + Jose C. Vitug; January 18, 2011 SC resolution with 60% MCQ + 40% essay (later reverted to bar-subject-specific essays at 40% in 2012); Justice Diosdado M. Peralta as 2014 bar chair; Raul C. Pangalangan November 25, 2011 column quote on '50-year-unchanged template'

Asterisk-divider postscript: SC unanimously voided Comelec's edict reducing TV-radio ads to aggregate-total 120-min TV + 180-min radio per national candidate; Feb 24, 2013 CJP column on 'draconian reduction' back-reference; Comelec accepted defeat graciously, will not ask for reconsideration
sub_topics: Conducted annually on four Sundays of October, bar examinations determine who will be blessed to carry exalted title of 'attorney-at-law' and allowed to practice law in all PH courts
PRC (Professional Regulation Commission) supervises entry to all professions in PH, except legal profession which is reserved by Constitution for SC
PRC-administered tests called 'licensure examinations'

Crucible of lawyers referred to as 'bar' exam, not because addicted to alcoholic drinks, but because only those who pass it may cross courtroom railing or bar that separates lawyers' table and judges' bench from seats used by general public
Hence lawyers collectively referred to as 'bar' and judges as 'bench'
Bar exam used to be attended by fanfare galore
Undergraduates held parades and motorcades complete with marching bands and cheering squads that accompanied school candidates' rides to exam venue
Gaudy practice banned by SC during last several years
Mystique explodes when results released â€” traditional and social media herald passers, complete with pictures of topnotchers plastered on front pages of newspapers and interviewed over radio and TV
Law schools take enormous pride in their bar placers and average passing rates
University of Cebu gifts its top 10 with brand-new cars
Most prestigious law firms and best-managed companies offer fantastic opportunities and compensation to top placers
CJP took bar exam in 1960 without this opening-day fanfare
On first day of exam, CJP had to wade alone in rain-induced, knee-deep flood on R. Papa Street, Sampaloc, Manila
Connected then Far Eastern University Hospital (where confined for intestinal flu) and University of the East (situs of exam)
Because of illness, CJP felt he may have failed first-day subjects and seriously thought of quitting
Dean Jovito R. Salonga pepped CJP up and urged him to go on

Recalling this incident in speech on Jan 22, 2008 at FEU, Salonga opined: 'CJ Panganiban could have landed first place (instead of sixth) had he not been sick and hospitalized during the first week of the 1960 bar exam'
CJP mentions this because he believes placing in top 10 not always reliable measure of achievement
In fact, classmate and CJP compared answers after each exam day; CJP was so impressed with friend's answers that he expected friend to fare better
Questions during CJP's time called mostly for essay answers and memory work

CJP thought classmate's citation of authorities was more impressive â€” but apparently examiner's evaluation was quite subjective and probably veered toward CJP's essayed opinions; hence CJP's grades higher
Subjective methodology and scope of bar exam have not improved much since CJP took it
Basic subjects the same, except for this year, two subjects â€” taxation and labor law â€” added
Methodology changed to 20% MCQ + 80% essay

Inquirer publisher Raul C. Pangalangan lamented in column Nov 25, 2011: 'The bar exams have been administered on basically the same template... The model remained unchanged at least for the last 50 years...'
To be fair, during last decade SC tried to improve bar exam

In resolution issued June 8, 2004 (Bar Matter 1161), Court favorably acted on reforms proposed by retired Justices Vicente V. Mendoza, Ameurfina M. Herrera and Jose C. Vitug (see CJP's April 22, 2007 column for details)

Court instituted more reforms in another resolution Jan 18, 2011, with MCQ at 60% of total exams to measure 'knowledge and recall of laws, doctrines and jurisprudence', plus 40% essay-type questions to test 'skill in writing in English, sorting out the relevant facts in a legal dispute... organizing [their] thoughts, constructing [their] arguments, and persuading readers to [their] point of view'
However, essays were not 'bar subject specific', igniting fiery howling from candidates
In 2012, SC reverted to 'bar subject specific' essays at 40% and MCQs at 60%
In 2013 exam, this was changed to 20% MCQ and 80% essay â€” same format for next month's exam, chaired by Justice Diosdado M. Peralta
Postscript: SC unanimously voided Comelec's edict reducing TV-radio ads during elections to 'aggregate total' of only 120 minutes for TV and 180 minutes for radio per national candidate

As CJP argued in Feb 24, 2013 column, this 'draconian reduction suppressed the people's right to know their candidates, and the aspirants' rights to inform the electorate of their qualifications and programs of government'
Good news: Comelec accepted defeat graciously and will not ask for reconsideration
Better news: it will, as it should, enforce strictly the legal limits on candidates' campaign [text cut off mid-sentence]

stances.claim: Bar mystique: the PRC supervises entry to all professions in the Philippines EXCEPT the legal profession, which is reserved by the Constitution for the SC; the PRC-administered tests are called 'licensure examinations' but the crucible of lawyers is referred to as the 'bar' exam â€” not because they are more addicted to alcoholic drinks, but because only those who pass it may cross the courtroom railing or bar that separates the lawyers' table and the judges' bench from the seats used by the general public; hence lawyers are collectively referred to as the 'bar' and judges as the 'bench'

The bar exam used to be attended by fanfare galore â€” undergraduates held parades and motorcades complete with marching bands and cheering squads accompanying their school candidates' rides to the exam venue; this gaudy practice had been banned by the SC during the last several years; the mystique explodes when results are released â€” traditional and social media herald passers, complete with topnotchers' pictures plastered on front pages and TV/radio interviews; law schools take enormous pride in their bar placers and passing rates; one institution, the University of Cebu, gifts its top 10 with brand-new cars; the most prestigious law firms and best-managed companies offer fantastic opportunities and compensation to top placers

Personal experience: CJ took the bar exam in 1960 without this opening-day fanfare â€” in fact, on the first day of the exam, he had to wade alone in rain-induced knee-deep flood on R. Papa Street, Sampaloc, Manila, which connected the then Far Eastern University Hospital (where he was confined for intestinal flu) and the University of the East (the situs of the exam); because of his illness, he felt he may have failed the first-day subjects and seriously thought of quitting â€” but Dean Jovito R. Salonga pepped him up and urged him to go on; recalling this incident in a speech on Jan 22, 2008 at FEU, Dr. Salonga opined: 'CJ Panganiban could have landed first place (instead of sixth) had he not been sick and hospitalized during the first week of the 1960 bar exam'

CJ mentions this because he believes placing in the top 10 is not always a reliable measure of achievement â€” in fact, a classmate and CJ compared their answers after each exam day; CJ was so impressed with his friend's answers that he expected him to fare better; the point is that the questions during CJ's time called mostly for essay answers and memory work; CJ thought his classmate's citation of authorities was more impressive than his own â€” but apparently the examiner's evaluation of their answers was quite subjective and probably veered toward CJ's essayed opinions; hence, CJ's grades were higher

Exam scope and methodology: the subjective methodology and scope of the bar exam have not improved much since CJ took it â€” the basic subjects are the same, except for this year, two subjects (taxation and labor law) were added, and the methodology changed to 20% MCQ + 80% essay; Inquirer publisher Raul C. Pangalangan lamented in his Nov 25, 2011 column that 'the bar exams have been administered on basically the same template... The model remained unchanged at least for the last 50 years...'

To be fair, during the last decade the SC tried to improve the bar exam: in Bar Matter 1161 (June 8, 2004), the Court favorably acted on reforms proposed by retired Justices Vicente V. Mendoza, Ameurfina M. Herrera and Jose C. Vitug (see CJ's April 22, 2007 column for details); the Court instituted more reforms in another resolution on January 18, 2011 with MCQ at 60% of total exams to measure 'knowledge and recall' plus 40% essay-type questions to test 'skill in writing in English, sorting out the relevant facts in a legal dispute... organizing thoughts, constructing arguments, and persuading readers'; however, the essays were not 'bar subject specific,' igniting fiery howling from candidates; in 2012 the SC reverted to 'bar subject specific' essays at 40% and MCQs at 60%; in the 2013 exam this was changed to 20% MCQ and 80% essay â€” the same format for next month's exam, chaired by Justice Diosdado M. Peralta

section labels (Bar mystique, My personal experience, Exam scope and methodology)
one_paragraph_summary: Panganiban opens the three-part bar-exam-and-legal-education trilogy with this first installment. Conducted annually on the four Sundays of October, the bar examinations determine who will be blessed to carry the exalted title of 'attorney-at-law' and be allowed to practice law in all courts in our country. Bar mystique: the Professional Regulation Commission (PRC) supervises the entry to all professions in the Philippines, except the legal profession which is reserved by the Constitution for the Supreme Court. The PRC-administered tests are called 'licensure examinations.' However, the crucible of lawyers is referred to as the 'bar' exam, not because they are more addicted to alcoholic drinks, but because only those who pass it may cross the courtroom railing or bar that separates the lawyers' table and the judges' bench, on one hand, from the seats used by the general public, on the other. Hence, lawyers are collectively referred to as the 'bar' and judges, the 'bench.' The bar exam used to be attended by fanfare galore. Undergraduates held parades and motorcades complete with marching bands and cheering squads that accompanied their school candidates' rides to the exam venue. This gaudy practice had been banned by the Supreme Court during the last several years. But the mystique explodes when the results are released. Traditional and social media herald the passers, complete with the pictures of the topnotchers plastered on the front pages of newspapers and interviewed over radio and television. Law schools take enormous pride in their bar placers and average passing rates. One institution, the University of Cebu, gifts its top 10 with brand-new cars. The most prestigious law firms and best-managed companies offer fantastic opportunities and compensation to the top placers. My personal experience: CJ took the bar exam in 1960 without this opening-day fanfare. In fact, on the first day of the exam, he had to wade alone in rain-induced, knee-deep flood on R. Papa Street, Sampaloc, Manila, which connected the then Far Eastern University Hospital (where he was confined for intestinal flu) and the University of the East, the situs of the exam. Because of his illness, he felt he may have failed the first-day subjects and seriously thought of quitting. But his dean, Dr. Jovito R. Salonga, pepped him up and urged him to go on. Recalling this incident in a speech on Jan 22, 2008, at FEU, Dr. Salonga opined that 'CJ Panganiban could have landed first place (instead of sixth) had he not been sick and hospitalized during the first week of the 1960 bar exam.' CJ mentions this because he believes that placing in the top 10 is not always a reliable measure of achievement. In fact, a classmate and CJ compared their answers after each exam day. CJ was so impressed with his friend's answers that he expected him to fare better than CJ. The point is that the questions during CJ's time called mostly for essay answers and memory work. CJ thought his classmate's citation of authorities was more impressive than his own. But apparently, the examiner's evaluation of their answers was quite subjective and probably veered toward CJ's essayed opinions. Hence, his grades were higher. Exam scope and methodology: the subjective methodology and scope of the bar exam have not improved much since CJ took it. The basic subjects are the same, except that for this year, two subjects â€” taxation and labor law â€” were added, and the methodology changed to 20 percent multiple choice questions (MCQ) and 80 percent essay. Inquirer publisher Raul C. Pangalangan lamented in his column on Nov 25, 2011, that 'the bar exams have been administered on basically the same template... The model remained unchanged at least for the last 50 years...' To be fair, however, let it be said that during the last decade, the Supreme Court tried to improve the bar exam. Thus, in a resolution issued on June 8, 2004 (Bar Matter 1161), the Court favorably acted on the reforms proposed by retired Justices Vicente V. Mendoza, Ameurfina M. Herrera and Jose C. Vitug (see CJ's April 22, 2007 column for details). Moreover, the Court instituted more reforms in another resolution on Jan 18, 2011, with MCQ at 60 percent of the total exams to measure the 'knowledge and recall of laws, doctrines and jurisprudence' of the candidates plus 40 percent essay-type questions to test their 'skill in writing in English, sorting out the relevant facts in a legal dispute... organizing [their] thoughts, constructing [their] arguments, and persuading readers to [their] point of view.' However, the essays were not 'bar subject specific,' igniting fiery howling from the candidates. In 2012, the Supreme Court reverted to the 'bar subject specific' essays at 40 percent and MCQs at 60 percent. In the 2013 exam, this was changed to 20 percent MCQ and 80 percent essay â€” the same format for next month's exam, chaired by Justice Diosdado M. Peralta. Postscript: the Supreme Court unanimously voided the Commission on Elections' edict reducing TV-radio ads during elections to an 'aggregate total' of only 120 minutes for TV and 180 minutes for radio per national candidate. As CJ argued in his Feb 24, 2013 column, this 'draconian reduction suppressed the people's right to know their candidates, and the aspirants' rights to inform the electorate of their qualifications and programs of government.' The good news, however, is that the Comelec accepted its defeat graciously and will not ask for reconsideration. And the better news is that it will, as it should, enforce strictly the legal limits on the candidates' campaign.
